title: Banbury cake
subtitle: Spiced currant pastry oval from Oxfordshire
excerpt: An oval pastry from Banbury in Oxfordshire, made by wrapping a spiced currant filling in flaky pastry and sealing it into an oval shape before baking. Distinct from an Eccles cake in its oval form, its slightly more generous proportions, and the higher spice content of the filling. The pastry is made with lard for flakiness, and a sugar glaze is applied hot from the oven.
sourceNotes: Banbury cakes are documented in English culinary literature from at least the seventeenth century as a speciality of Banbury, Oxfordshire. Richard Briggs, The English Art of Cookery (1788, public domain and available at Project Gutenberg), describes a spiced currant cake from Banbury. The oval pastry format with a currant, peel, and spice filling is described in English domestic cookery and regional food guides published before 1928.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze unbaked, assembled. Bake from frozen, adding 5 to 8 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Assemble and refrigerate unbaked for up to 24 hours. The filling can be made up to 3 days ahead.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 190°C. High heat gives the pastry its characteristic quick puff and golden colour. Bake for 20 to 25 minutes.

glossaryTerms.definition: Placing a portion of spiced currant filling in the centre of a pastry round, gathering the edges up and pressing them together to seal, then turning the parcel over seam-side down and gently shaping it into an oval with the hands. Score three diagonal slits across the top before baking to allow steam to escape and to give the traditional Banbury appearance. A Banbury cake should be plumper and wider than an Eccles cake, more like a small cushion than a flat disc.

content.text: Banbury cakes are made with puff pastry (or the rough puff method using lard), a very spiced currant filling, and a slightly different shape from an Eccles cake.
is the distinguishing feature. This version uses ready-made puff pastry to focus on the filling and shaping, which can be done in the time it takes to preheat the oven.

Mix the currants, mixed peel, melted butter, dark brown sugar, mixed spice, cinnamon, and rum together. Leave to macerate for 30 minutes. Roll the puff pastry to about 3 mm thick and cut into 12 cm rounds. Place a heaped tablespoon of filling in the centre of each round. Gather the pastry edges up and press firmly together to seal, then turn over seam-side down. Shape gently into an oval and score three diagonal slits across the top with a sharp knife.

Place on a lined baking tray and refrigerate for 30 minutes. Heat the oven to 210°C conventional (190°C fan). Brush the cakes with beaten egg and sprinkle with caster sugar. Bake for 20 to 25 minutes until deep golden and puffed. Cool on a rack for 10 minutes before eating. Best eaten warm.
